title: C# SOLID — Paper 6
description: Covers LSP refactoring, ISP concepts, ISP refactoring, DIP concepts, DIP implementation, Repository Pattern, Code Smells & SOLID Violations, Refactoring Techniques, Unit Testing & SOLID, and SOLID Real C# Scenarios.

questions.text: ST10-1: Constructor injection in C# DIP implementation means:

options.text: The class creates its own dependencies inside the constructor using `new`.
Dependencies are provided to a class through its constructor parameters as abstractions (interfaces).
The constructor calls a static factory to resolve dependencies.
Dependencies are injected after construction using setter properties.

questions.text: ST20-1: Given the code snippet: `public class OrderService { private readonly EmailService _email = new EmailService(); }` — which SOLID principle is violated?

options.text: SRP — OrderService should not have any fields.
DIP — OrderService depends directly on a concrete EmailService rather than an abstraction.
OCP — EmailService cannot be extended.
ISP — EmailService has too many methods.

questions.text: ST16-12: When implementing the Repository pattern with Entity Framework Core, which statement is most accurate?

options.text: DbContext already acts as a repository, so adding a separate repository layer is always unnecessary.
Wrapping DbContext in a repository interface allows swapping EF Core for a different ORM or a fake in tests without changing business logic.
Repositories should include DbContext as a public property.
Repository methods should always return IQueryable<T> to push all query logic to callers.

questions.text: ST08-7: In C#, explicit interface implementation is used to:

options.text: Make all interface methods public on the class.
Implement an interface method without exposing it publicly on the class, so it is only accessible via the interface reference.
Allow the class to implement the same method name for two interfaces with different signatures simultaneously.
Both b and c.

questions.text: ST09-5: Which of the following is a direct consequence of violating DIP by instantiating concrete classes inside high-level modules?

options.text: The code compiles faster.
High-level modules become tightly coupled to low-level details, making substitution and unit testing difficult.
The program uses less memory at runtime.
The high-level module gains extra capabilities from the low-level module.

questions.text: ST19-5: When SOLID principles are followed, why does mocking become easier?

options.text: Because mock frameworks only work with sealed classes.
Because dependencies are represented as interfaces or abstract types, which mocking frameworks can substitute easily.
Because SOLID-compliant classes always have public setters.
Because mocking is not needed when SOLID principles are applied.

questions.text: ST06-14: When a derived class raises an exception type that the base class does not declare, this can violate LSP because:

options.text: Callers expect only the exceptions defined by the base class contract; unexpected exceptions break substitutability.
Exceptions are always LSP violations regardless of context.
The derived class should never throw any exceptions.
This is not an LSP issue; exception types are irrelevant to substitutability.

questions.text: ST20-2: A `Logger` class has methods: `LogInfo()`, `LogWarning()`, `LogError()`, `SendEmail()`, `WriteToDatabase()`. Which principle is most clearly violated?

options.text: OCP
SRP — Logger has more than one reason to change (logging format, email transport, database schema).
LSP
ISP

questions.text: ST07-11: Which of the following correctly refactors a fat `IVehicle` interface (Drive, Fly, Sail) following ISP?

options.text: Add default implementations for Fly and Sail in IVehicle.
Split into IDriveable, IFlyable, and ISailable; a Car implements only IDriveable.
Mark Fly and Sail as optional using attributes.
Create a Vehicle base class with virtual methods.

questions.text: ST17-11: 'Message Chains' (a.b().c().d()) is a code smell related to which principle?

options.text: DIP — too many concrete dependencies.
Law of Demeter — a class should only talk to its immediate collaborators, not navigate deep object graphs.
LSP — the chain may break substitutability.
OCP — the chain cannot be extended.

questions.text: ST10-2: The `IServiceCollection` extension method `AddTransient<TService, TImplementation>()` in ASP.NET Core registers a service so that:

options.text: One instance is shared for the lifetime of the application.
A new instance is created every time the service is requested.
One instance is shared per HTTP request.
The implementation is resolved lazily on first use only.

questions.text: ST18-9: 'Decompose Conditional' refactoring involves:

options.text: Removing all if-statements from a class.
Extracting the condition, then-part, and else-part of a complex if-else into well-named methods to improve clarity.
Combining multiple conditions into a single boolean expression.
Replacing conditions with try-catch blocks.

questions.text: ST09-6: A 'Service Locator' is sometimes contrasted with DIP-based injection. What is the key criticism of Service Locator?

options.text: It is too slow at runtime.
It hides dependencies inside method bodies rather than declaring them explicitly, making them harder to discover and test.
It only supports singleton lifetimes.
It cannot work with interfaces.

questions.text: ST19-6: A 'Spy' test double differs from a Mock in that:

options.text: A Spy provides canned responses, while a Mock throws exceptions.
A Spy wraps a real object and records calls made to it, while a Mock is entirely fabricated.
A Spy is set up before the test; a Mock is set up after.
A Spy can only be used with static methods.

questions.text: ST20-3: A `ShapeRenderer` class has a method with `if (shape is Circle) { ... } else if (shape is Rectangle) { ... }`. Which principle does this violate?

options.text: DIP
OCP — adding a new shape requires modifying ShapeRenderer.
LSP
ISP

questions.text: ST06-15: Which of the following statements about LSP and interfaces is correct?

options.text: Interfaces cannot be involved in LSP violations because they have no implementation.
Implementations of an interface must honour the behavioral contract implied by the interface, not just its method signatures.
Any class that compiles with an interface is automatically LSP compliant.
LSP only applies to abstract class hierarchies, not interface hierarchies.

questions.text: ST10-3: In ASP.NET Core, when a service is registered with `AddSingleton<T>()`, its instance is:

options.text: Created once per HTTP request and shared within that request.
Created once for the application lifetime and shared across all requests and users.
Created every time it is requested.
Created only on the first HTTP request.

questions.text: ST17-12: 'Parallel Inheritance Hierarchies' is a code smell where:

options.text: Two completely independent inheritance trees exist in the same project.
Every time you add a subclass in one hierarchy, you must add a corresponding subclass in another hierarchy, indicating tight coupling.
Two classes inherit from the same base class.
A hierarchy has more than five levels of inheritance.

questions.text: ST08-8: A `IRepository<T>` interface includes `GetById`, `GetAll`, `Add`, `Update`, `Delete`, and `ExecuteRawSql`. A reporting service only needs `GetAll`. ISP suggests:

options.text: The reporting service implements all methods, throwing exceptions for unused ones.
Split off an IReadRepository<T> with just GetById and GetAll so read-only consumers depend on a narrower contract.
Add an IsReadOnly flag to IRepository<T>.
Make all write methods optional using attributes.

questions.text: ST19-7: What does 'Arrange, Act, Assert' (AAA) mean in the context of unit testing?

options.text: Authenticate, Authorize, Audit.
Set up the test conditions (Arrange), execute the unit under test (Act), then verify the outcome (Assert).
Add, Apply, Assess code changes.
Abstract, Aggregate, Annotate test cases.

questions.text: ST20-4: A class `EmailSender` has a public method `Send()` that constructs an `SmtpClient` internally. What is the DIP-compliant refactoring?

options.text: Make SmtpClient a static field of EmailSender.
Introduce an IEmailTransport interface and inject it into EmailSender's constructor so the transport mechanism can be swapped or mocked.
Subclass SmtpClient inside EmailSender.
Move the SmtpClient construction into a separate private method.

questions.text: ST09-7: In a DIP-compliant architecture, which layer typically defines the interfaces (abstractions)?

options.text: The infrastructure layer (e.g., data access).
The high-level domain/application layer, so that low-level infrastructure depends on the domain's abstractions.
The presentation layer.
A shared utilities library.

questions.text: ST10-4: Which of the following is a valid way to implement DIP without a DI container in C#?

options.text: Using `new` inside every class that needs a dependency.
Manually constructing the object graph at the application's composition root, passing abstractions via constructors.
Using static factory methods within each dependency class.
Using reflection to discover and instantiate all dependencies at startup.

questions.text: ST16-13: In the Repository pattern, what is the preferred return type for a method that might or might not find an entity?

options.text: Always returning a default-constructed entity to avoid null.
Returning `T?` (nullable reference type) or a Result<T> type to explicitly communicate the absence of a value.
Throwing a NotFoundException if the entity is not found.
Returning an empty List<T> even for single-entity queries.

questions.text: ST07-12: Which of the following statements about ISP and cohesion is correct?

options.text: ISP reduces cohesion by splitting methods across too many interfaces.
ISP promotes high cohesion by ensuring each interface groups only closely related methods that a specific type of client needs.
ISP and cohesion are unrelated concerns.
High cohesion leads to fat interfaces that violate ISP.

questions.text: ST20-5: A `UserController` directly instantiates `new UserRepository()` and `new EmailService()`. Which principles are violated?

options.text: OCP and LSP
DIP and SRP — it depends on concrete types and mixes controller and service-creation concerns.
ISP and LSP
OCP and ISP

questions.text: ST19-8: Why does a class that violates SRP tend to have low unit testability?

options.text: SRP violations cause compilation errors that prevent testing.
Mixed concerns mean the class has many hard-coded dependencies, making it hard to isolate and test any single behavior.
SRP violations always cause NullReferenceExceptions in test environments.
SRP-violating classes cannot be mocked.

questions.text: ST18-10: 'Introduce Null Object' refactoring replaces null checks with:

options.text: Throwing ArgumentNullException everywhere null was expected.
A special object implementing the interface with do-nothing or default behavior, eliminating null reference checks.
A nullable wrapper type such as Nullable<T>.
An optional parameter with a default value of null.

questions.text: ST06-16: An abstract class `Animal` has method `MakeSound()`. A `Fish` subclass inherits `Animal` and throws `NotSupportedException` from `MakeSound()`. The best LSP fix is:

options.text: Wrap the call in a try-catch in all callers.
Remove Fish from the Animal hierarchy; use composition or a separate ISoundMaker interface implemented only by sound-making animals.
Make Animal sealed so Fish cannot inherit from it.
Add an CanMakeSound boolean property to Animal.

questions.text: ST20-6: In C#, which code pattern indicates a violation of both OCP and SRP in a single method?

options.text: A method that delegates to an injected strategy object.
A method that both validates input AND applies business rules AND formats output using a chain of if-else statements that grow with every new feature.
A method that returns a result from an injected repository.
A method that calls two collaborating services injected via DI.
